IMPACT.justification: The paper defines a novel, well-motivated Dual Machine Comprehension task and provides a practical algorithm to generate challenging decoy captions, instantiated as a large-scale MCIC dataset based on COCO (Section 3). It supplies human performance measurements (e.g., 81.1% single-rater accuracy) to set an upper bound and strong baseline/competitive models (Vec2seq+FFNN) showing that comprehension accuracy correlates with image-captioning performance (Section 5.3). These contributions produce a simple, interpretable evaluation metric and a reusable dataset that are likely to influence future multimodal representation and evaluation work, opening clear research directions without being wholly transformative.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — the paper proposes a new multimodal vision-language task and a large-scale COCO-based dataset (MCIC) with a principled decoy-generation method (Section 3) that directly targets the interests of NLP/ML communities working on grounding, VQA and image captioning. It includes human evaluation (Section 3.4), standard baselines and neural models (Inception-v3 features, GRU Vec2seq+FFNN) and uses accepted metrics (accuracy, CIDEr, ROUGE; Sections 5.1 and 5.3), matching the methodological and evaluation standards of top venues. The work is positioned against relevant prior datasets/tasks (VQA, Visual7W, image captioning) and explores multitask training, making it well targeted to the venue audience and scope.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a generally logical structure (task description → dataset creation → experiments) and states main contributions early (see opening paragraph). However, the provided text contains many formatting and presentation issues—repeated duplicate sentences and reference lines, LaTeX/footnote artifacts (†), and missing referenced items (Eq.4, Table 3) that interrupt readability. Notation and naming are sometimes inconsistent or unclear (e.g., “FFNNargmax 1..52-class”, mix of DMC/MCIC), and a few grammatical slips ("overly simplistic as it is provides") further reduce clarity. These problems make parts hard to follow despite adequate organization and clear high-level goals.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper evaluates several reasonable baselines (Inception+PV random-projection baseline, FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and reports accuracy and standard captioning metrics (CIDEr/ROUGE) on COCO-derived MCIC with human upper-bound (Section 5.1 and 3.4), using the same data and evaluation scripts. It discusses related work and cites many relevant vision–language papers (Hodosh et al., Karpathy & Fei-Fei, Antol et al., Show-Attend-and-Tell, etc.) and analyzes model differences (e.g. λgen trade-off in Section 5.3). However, it does not apply or report results from a broader set of existing multimodal retrieval/alignment or recent VQA-style embedding methods as direct baselines on the new DMC task (only discusses differences to Hodosh et al.), so some important comparative baselines are missing.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides substantial experimental work: construction and public release of the MCIC dataset with an explicit decoy-generation algorithm (uses PV and simSURF/BLEU), human evaluation (1- and 3-rater results), multiple baselines (Inception+PV, FFNN, Vec2seq, Vec2seq+FFNN) and detailed training hyperparameters. It includes sensitivity analysis over the λgen multitask weight showing trade-offs between comprehension accuracy and captioning (CIDEr/ROUGE), and compares generation and classification approaches. Coverage of related work is extensive with many citations. However, some important analyses are missing or deferred (detailed ablations on decoy-difficulty settings and model components, per-error analyses and failure cases are only briefly mentioned or left to the supplement), and not all reproduction details of the MC-IC algorithm are present in the provided excerpt, so I rate it solid but with minor gaps.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a notable new formulation (the Dual Machine Comprehension task) together with an automated, controllable decoy-generation algorithm that combines paragraph-vector similarity and surface (BLEU) similarity to produce challenging caption distractors (sections 3.1 and MC-IC description). It also releases a large-scale MCIC instantiation on COCO and provides human upper bounds, clearly distinguishing itself from prior ranking work (Hodosh et al.) by emphasizing controlled, hard decoys and by using PV+linguistic-surface criteria. The modeling (Vec2seq+FFNN multitask linking comprehension and caption generation) is a sensible, moderately novel extension but largely builds on existing seq2seq and FFNN components, so the overall contribution is notable rather than revolutionary.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear claims (a decoy-generation procedure, a COCO-derived MCIC dataset, human upper-bounds, and models showing a positive correlation between comprehension accuracy and captioning performance) and provides supporting evidence: a described decoy-selection approach using paragraph vectors + surface BLEU, human evaluation results (81.1% single-rater accuracy; 82.8% ≥2/3 agreement on 1,000 dev instances), and experiments varying λgen with reported accuracy/CIDEr trade-offs (e.g. ~59% test accuracy at λgen=0.1 and high CIDEr at λgen=4). Mathematical content is minimal (loss Eq.4 is referenced but not shown), and experimental methodology is generally sound (reasonable baselines, Inception-v3 features, human evaluation protocol), but the excerpt omits full algorithm/equation details, lacks mention of statistical significance or multiple random runs, and gives limited ablation/complete quantitative tables in the provided text. These are minor gaps that do not invalidate the main conclusions.
